rules.text_en: A directive line starts with `^` and contains an instruction from sender to receiver. The decoder must preserve the `^` and the rest of the line verbatim. The receiver should treat the line as actionable.
Plain text lines (no `^` prefix) are narrative content, preserved verbatim. They are not instructions.
Messages are sent as a single unit. Do not add chunk headers or separators like `[part X/Y]` or `---END---`.

The sender may write any line in minimal form: omit punctuation (.,:;!?-) and capitalization except where required for numeric literals, unit symbols, or alphanumeric identifiers. The decoder must reconstruct the intended meaning, inferring appropriate punctuation and capitalization as needed.

In numeric literals, a leading zero before a decimal point may be omitted only if the value is less than 1. The decimal point (`.`) must be preserved exactly. Numeric literals must preserve all punctuation that affects value, including colons in times and leading zeros. The decoder must interpret these exactly.

When writing technical identifiers (command-line flags, API endpoints, zone names, license plates, etc.), the sender must preserve all punctuation marks (hyphens, underscores, slashes, equals) exactly as they appear in the original source. Do not replace them with spaces or omit them.

The sender must preserve all core contextual facts from the original message: named entities (people, organizations), event names, venues, key constraints, deadlines, attendee counts, capacity limits, explicit stakes, penalty clauses, key quantities, and explicit times. These must appear as plain text lines (no `^` prefix) before the directives they justify. Omitting such context renders the message non-compliant.

Within a message, the sender may define a short alias for a long or frequently repeated string. To define, write `@name=full_text` on its own line. Thereafter, `@name` may be used in place of the full text. The decoder must substitute the full text. Aliases are local to the message and case-sensitive. The sender should alias when the estimated token savings are positive: definition tokens + (uses × alias-use tokens) < uses × original tokens. As a guideline, alias when original string repeats ≥4 times, regardless of length, provided the alias definition ≤ original tokens × 2.

When the message consists primarily of instructions or actionable items, the sender should write each as a directive line (starting with `^`). Use plain text lines only for explanatory context that cannot be framed as an instruction. For directive lines, the sender may use positional slots to imply function words. Recommended order: action, object, condition, deadline, rationale. Omit prepositions (at, before, for, to) and articles where the slot order conveys the relationship. Decoder must infer the intended meaning.
Remove all rejected rules from the rulebook. Only adopted rules remain. This reduces the rulebook’s token count and clarifies the language for a fresh agent.
